What is a Real-Time Digital Twin? | 2024

Digital twins have been revolutionizing manufacturing, systems analysis, customer service, medical industries, marketing, and so much more. The value of digital twins - especially manufacturing and processes-related digital twins - lies in their ability to accelerate decision-making, promote sustainability, avoid critical mishaps, increase productivity, and more.Â

Real-time digital twins, with their ability to adapt to new data constantly, are only adding to these benefits. Weâll explore what a real-time digital twin is and how organizations can benefit from real-time digital twin technology.

What is a Real-Time Digital Twin?

Digital twins are digital representations of real-world objects, people, or processes used to observe past and present data, simulate potential future outcomes, accelerate holistic understanding, and more.

Real-time digital twins receive data in a continuous stream, tracking dynamic information about a physical device to allow for optimized and constantly up-to-date analysis. These real-time digital twins help identify problems and speed up response times to such problems, and they allow for on-the-fly adjustments to reach optimal efficiency.Â

For our purposes, weâll focus on âpeopleâ-type digital twins, which are digital representations of human beings. When these digital twins have real-time capabilities, they can be created instantly and can interact with humans in real time.

An example of this would be Tavusâ talking-head digital twin. AI models at Tavus can generate your very own digital twin for instant video generation at scale.

Real-Time Digital Twin vs Traditional Digital Twin

Real-time and traditional digital twins are differentiated by how they make use of the data that creates and sustains them. Conventional digital twins collect data about an object and then store that data for later analysis, whether to find and address problems or to conduct simulations.

Real-time digital twins, on the other hand, utilize the data they receive immediately, allowing for in-the-moment use with up-to-date information. For example, you might generate your digital twin for use as a live chat avatar with real-time processing of customer preferences, creating a more engaging and personalized experience.

How Does Real-Time Digital Twin Technology Work?Â

Digital twin technology gathers data from physical sensors (or, in the case of video-based digital twins, from sample videos) and uses the data to replicate the object, person, or process in a digital environment. Typically, digital twins store data and then perform analytics, since traditional methods of analysis have difficulty meeting the computational demands of real-time digital twins.Â

Thatâs why real-time digital twins require scalable software architecture so they can analyze streaming data on the fly and provide faster responses. One method to address this need is the use of in-memory computing, which can deliver messages to digital twins within milliseconds.

Real-Time Digital Twin Examples & Use CasesÂ

A wide variety of industries already use digital twin technology. Weâll explore the importance of real-time integration for improving the effectiveness of digital twins in each of these applications.

Marketing & SalesÂ

Real-time digital twin technology for people-type digital twins learns the nuances of your facial expressions, movements, and voice rapidly. This allows your digital avatar to reflect you with a high level of precision. That speed enables personalized video generation at scale.

Another important feature you want from your digital twin generator is the ability to use your digital twin to create thousands of marketing and sales videos immediately while retaining that highly-realistic replica of your image and voice.Â

Thatâs why Tavus is an excellent choice for your real-time digital twin. With scaled personalization options and unlimited video generation, you can create engaging sales videos to improve your conversion rates.Â

Generate personalized marketing videos with Tavusâs real-time digital twin capabilities!

ConstructionÂ

Digital twins in the construction industry help streamline the planning, budget, design, and actual construction phases. Construction digital twins are virtual representations of buildings or infrastructure projects, using data from sensors, BIM models, and more to optimize performance and improve decision-making.Â

If you make that digital twin real-time, with constant streams of data from the construction site, you can identify problems as they come up, rather than having to fix issues later and extend construction time.Â

And once a building is constructed, its digital twin can monitor the entire structure, enabling in-the-moment repairs down the line to avoid safety issues or delayed solutions.

Education & TrainingÂ

When it comes to manufacturing, real-time digital twins can help provide manufacturing students with the opportunity to practice their skills with real-time simulations from actual systems. Digital twins give students the opportunity for hands-on learning without the risk of causing real-life problems.

Training and educational videos can also benefit from real-time, people-type digital twins. Platforms like Tavus can generate thousands of videos without the labor and long wait times of traditional recording. Educators can also choose to implement AI video-based chatbots where a digital twin can interact with human users in real time, providing an engaging chat experience.

ManufacturingÂ

Digital twins in manufacturing are used to replicate processes or systems. Manufacturers use these replicas to analyze, monitor, design, or optimize their processes without having to work in the field or conduct expensive physical trials.

With real-time digital twins, manufacturers can extend the usefulness of these replicas to allow for continuous monitoring of plants or other digital manufacturing processes. They can identify areas for potential optimization, safety issues, and more and conduct the needed repairs or changes immediately, saving time and money and increasing efficiency.

Benefits of Real-Time Digital Twins

Letâs explore the benefits of real-time integration for digital twins.

Enhances PersonalizationÂ

A primary benefit of real-time digital twins is the development of conversational AI, which uses natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning to understand the context and intent of user inquiries. The ability to process human input and respond in a natural, conversational way enhances personalization and encourages connection between brands and their audiences.Â

Increases Accuracy & ReliabilityÂ

A traditional digital twin is still a static model if it does not implement real-time data and analysis, creating the possibility that it may be accurate at one point and then potentially obsolete the next. A constant stream of data allows real-time digital twins to evolve alongside their physical counterparts with precision.

Improves Predictive CapabilitiesÂ

The moment-by-moment updates provided by real-time digital twins allows for immediate feedback on problems and trends. Such swift insights allow for improved predictive capabilities and provide opportunities to fix problems before or as they happen.

Improved predictive capabilities also benefit people-type digital twins. With the ability to predict where a conversation will go or what types of responses are most common for certain types of human input, real-time digital twins can improve how natural their interactions are.

Saves Time & MoneyÂ Â

For traditional digital twins, even the best infrastructure needs time for data to travel from its source to processing units, causing delays. With real-time digital twins, that travel and processing time is cut down to milliseconds, saving organizations time. And faster speeds mean problems can be addressed sooner, saving money on delayed repairs and time for systems to be shut down.

Challenges of Real-Time Digital Twins

Implementing real-time capabilities in digital twins introduces some challenges. Transmission delays still exist, even with the best infrastructure, and complex or high-volume processing can also introduce low latency. Add in network congestion as a result of high traffic or limited bandwidth, and you may be left with even further delays.

Thatâs why itâs so important to choose the right technology for your real-time digital twin needs. For people-type digital twins, Tavus is one of the top choices on the market. How to Create a Real-Time Digital Twin for VideoÂ

Once you have your template video, select the words or phrases that you want personalized for each individual video message. Provide Tavus with each individualâs data (including name, preferences, and personalized CTAs), and let Tavus do the rest.

Step 3: Generate unlimited videos with a real-time digital twinÂ

Weâll use your digital twin and Tavusâ text-to-speech capabilities to create thousands of high-quality videos for each and every one of your customers! No need for lengthy editing processes or costly recording sessions.

Make Your Own Real-Time Digital Twin With TavusÂ

If you want to generate your own real-time digital twin for video generation at scale, youâre in the right place. Tavusâ generative AI allows you to create unlimited videos in minutes so you can make digital twin videos with realistic, real-time interactions.Â

Top 5 Benefits of Digital Twin Technology Revolutionising Construction
======================================================================

Digital twin technology is transforming industries, and construction is no exception. But what is a digital twin? At its core, a digital twin is a virtual replica of a physical asset or system, such as a building, infrastructure, or equipment, which is dynamically updated with real-time data. This powerful technology allows construction professionals to create a digital mirror of their projects, enabling them to visualise, simulate, and analyse various components throughout the asset’s lifecycle.

The use of digital twin technology in construction is revolutionising how projects are planned, built, and managed. Below are five transformative benefits of implementing digital twin technology in construction.

1. Enhanced Project Visualisation and Simulation
------------------------------------------------

One of the most significant advantages of digital twin technology in construction is its ability to enhance project visualisation and simulation. Unlike traditional 2D blueprints or static 3D models, digital twins offer dynamic, real-time models that evolve with the project. This provides stakeholders with a comprehensive view of the construction process, from the design phase through to completion and operation.

Moreover, advanced 3D visualisation enables clients to “experience” the project before it’s built. This fosters better client engagement, offering a tangible preview of the finished structure and ensuring that expectations are met, if not exceeded.

2. Improved Collaboration and Communication
-------------------------------------------

Construction projects often involve numerous stakeholders, including architects, engineers, contractors, and clients. Digital twins technology significantly enhances collaboration by providing a unified platform where all parties can access the same real-time data and insights. This centralisation of information minimises misunderstandings, enhances communication, and ensures that everyone is aligned on the project’s progress.

Decision-making becomes more data-driven. Stakeholders can collaborate on simulations to test various construction approaches, identify risks early, and agree on the best course of action.

3. Better Predictive Maintenance and Asset Management
-----------------------------------------------------

Digital twins are not only valuable during the construction phase, but they also offer immense benefits for long-term asset management. Once a building or infrastructure is completed, the digital twin continues to function as a live model that tracks the asset’s performance over time. This makes it easier for facility managers and building owners to monitor and manage operations, maintenance, and repairs.

Additionally, by integrating with Building Information Modelling (BIM) systems, digital twins ensure that accurate information is always available for future renovations or upgrades.

4. Increased Efficiency and Reduced Costs
-----------------------------------------

Digital twins contribute to significant cost savings and increased efficiency throughout the construction lifecycle. By allowing real-time analysis and simulation, construction teams can identify potential issues early on, reducing the likelihood of costly errors during the build phase. The insights gathered from digital twins enable better resource allocation, helping to minimise material waste, optimise labour costs, and streamline workflows.

Digital twins provide the ability to track inventory, materials, and equipment in real time, ensuring that resources are used efficiently and on schedule.

5. Sustainability and Environmental Impact Reduction
----------------------------------------------------

Sustainability is becoming a top priority in construction, and digital twin technology is playing a crucial role in helping the industry reduce its environmental footprint. By optimising resource use, improving energy efficiency, and minimising waste, digital twins contribute to greener building practices.

The real-time data provided by digital twins also helps construction teams track and reduce waste during the building process. By having precise insights into material usage and construction progress, teams can prevent over-ordering materials, reducing excess waste that would otherwise end up in landfills.

*   •Digital Twins will combine with more technologies, such as augmented reality (AR) and AI for better connections, insights, and analytics. 

Abstract
--------

Applications of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been growing at an exponential rate, and it is transforming the way businesses operate. In the past few years, [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leveraged vital [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is predicted that the technology will expand to more applications, use cases, and industries. The purpose of this paper is to do a literature review and explore how Digital Twins streamline intelligent automation in different industries. This paper defines the concept, highlights the evolution and development of Digital Twins, reviews its key enabling technologies, examines its trends and challenges, and explores its applications in different industries.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Digital Twin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is attracting attention from practitioners and scholars alike. Today, the technology is used across many industries to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. In 2019, a Gartner survey revealed that Digital Twins were entering mainstream use by organizations. It predicted that 75 percent of [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) organizations also use [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or plan to use it by 2020 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b2). Gartner also estimates that by 2027, over 40 percent of large companies worldwide will be using Digital Twin in their projects to increase revenue [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b3).

Moreover, Global Market Insight estimated that the Digital Twin market size estimated in 2022 at $8 billion is expected to grow at around 25 percent [Compound Annual Growth Rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about Compound Annual Growth Rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAGR) from 2023 to 2032 [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b4). Finally, according to another recent report by global technology research, the Digital Twin market is set to grow by nearly $32 billion from 2021 to 2026 [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b5). In addition, according to a 2022 report, nearly 60 percent of executives across a broad spectrum of industry plan to incorporate Digital Twins within their operations by 2028 [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6).

Digital Twin is a cutting-edge technology that has revolutionized the industry by mirroring almost every facet of a product, process, or service. It has the potential to replicate everything in the physical world in the digital space and provide engineers with feedback from the virtual world [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7). As a result, the technology enables companies to quickly detect and solve physical problems, design and build better products, and realize value and benefits faster than previously possible. Furthermore, the Digital Twin technology enables businesses to improve business processes and performance [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b8).

The research started by developing key concepts based on a narrative literature review in the Scopus database and Google Scholar and searched for papers that included Digital Twin or Digital Twins in the title. Articles were selected from Journal articles, research articles, conference publications, research reports, or scientific encyclopedias. We reviewed the documents to find answers to the following questions: What is a Digital Twin, what technologies are used, what are the current applications of Digital Twins in different industries, and what are the challenges and opportunities facing this growing technology? We selected research papers that defined the concept, reviewed its related technologies, and highlighted its applications and trends in different industries. Section[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec2) provides the main perspectives and definitions of Digital Twins in literature. Section[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec3) reviews four enabling technologies of the Digital Twins. Section[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec4) studies Digital Twin applications and use cases of different industries. Section[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec5) highlights the challenges and opportunities of this technology. Finally, Section[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec6) provides a summary and conclusions. Section 7 provides references.

2. Background and definition
----------------------------

In 2003 Professor Grieves of the University of Michigan introduced the concept of [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a total [product lifecycle management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/product-lifecycle-management "Learn more about product lifecycle management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") course. It is also known as a digital mirror and digital mapping. Since then, its definition has continued to evolve as several scholars have provided varied definitions of this technology [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b14). Encyclopedia of Production Engineering states that “_The Digital Twin is a representation of an active unique “product” which can be a real device, object, machine, service, intangible asset, or a system consisting of a product and its related services”_[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12)_._ In general, the Digital Twin is defined as virtual representations of [physical objects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical objects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the lifecycle that can be understood, learned, and reasoned with real-time data or a simulation model that acquires data from the field and triggers the operation of physical devices [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b16).

Furthermore, Digital Twin was defined as the convergence between physical and virtual products [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b18). Fu et al. [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7) thought of the Digital Twin as a real-time digital representation of a physical object. They are remotely connected to real objects and provide rich representations of these objects. They go beyond static product designs, like CAD models, but comprise dynamic behavior [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b20). A virtual replica of a real-world asset is obtained through constant data transmission allowing the digital version of the object to exist simultaneously with the physical one [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17). Digital Twin uses [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology to mine hidden and effective data and to improve the intelligence and applicability of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), especially for quick identification and evaluation of design flaws [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b21).

Finally, Kritzinger et al. [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13) defined Digital Twin, within the field of manufacturing, based on the data integration level, which can be achieved between the physical product and its virtual representation. He identified three levels of integration, The Digital Model, Digital Shadow and the Digital Twin ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig1)) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b22).

A historic early application of Digital Twin technology is when NASA engineers used a simulator, a twin of the command module, and a separate twin of the module’s [electrical system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrical-system "Learn more about electrical system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to remedy and save Apollo 13 in 1970. NASA engineers completed the process in under two hours and saved the lives of the three astronauts on board. This was an extraordinary early application of this technology, and the technology has only matured since then [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b23). Today, NASA uses Digital Twins to develop next-generation vehicles and aircraft.

The concept of Digital Twins is not new. However, Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. It is predicted that Digital Twins will be combined with more technologies such as speech capabilities, [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [IoT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about IoT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [artificial intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about artificial intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI). As a result, Gartner included Digital Twins on its list of top 10 technology trends for 2017 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Gartner also predicted that half of the large industrial firms to use Digital Twins in crucial [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by 2021 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Finally, MarketsandMarkets research predicted rapid growth for the Digital Twin technology within the next few years, thanks to rising interest in the manufacturing industry to reduce cost and improve [supply chain operations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/supply-chain-operation "Learn more about supply chain operations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, the market for Digital Twin technology was valued at $6.9 billion in 2022. However, it is expected to reach $73.5 billion by 2027- a [CAGR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about CAGR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of more than 60 percent [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b25).

3. Digital twin technologies
----------------------------

The three main aspects of Digital Twins are data acquisition, [data modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-modeling "Learn more about data modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and data application [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). Digital Twin uses four technologies to collect and store real-time data, obtain information to provide valuable insights, and create a digital representation of a [physical object](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical object from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These technologies include the Internet of Things (IoT), [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI), [Extended Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR), and Cloud ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig2)). In addition, Digital Twin uses a particular technology, depending on the application type, to a greater or lesser extent.

*   1._Internet of Things (IoT):_ IoT refers to a giant network of connected “things”. The connection is between things–things, people–things, or people–people [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Digital Twins use IoT as its primary technology in every application. By 2027, more than 90 percent of all IoT platforms will have Digital Twinning capability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6). IoT uses sensors to collect data from real-world objects. The data transmitted by IoT is used to create a digital duplication of a physical object. The digital version then can then be analyzed, manipulated, and optimized. IoT constantly updates data and helps Digital Twin applications create a real-time virtual representation of a physical object. Therefore, every Digital Twin application uses IoT as a primary technology. 
*   2._[Cloud Computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/cloud-computing "Learn more about Cloud Computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_: Cloud computing refers to delivering hosted services over the Internet. The technology efficiently stores and accesses data over the Internet [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Cloud computing provides Digital Twins with data computing technology and cloud data storage technology. Cloud computing allows Digital Twin, with large volumes of data, to store data in the virtual Cloud and easily access the required information from any location. Cloud computing enables Digital Twins to effectively reduce the [computation time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computation-time "Learn more about computation time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of complex systems and overcome the difficulties of storing large amounts of data [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b28). 
*   3.[Artificial Intelligence (AI)](https://softengi.com/expertise/artificial-intelligence/): As a discipline of computer science, AI seeks to mimic the basis of intelligence to create a new intelligent machine capable of responding like human-to-human intelligence. Areas of AI study include Robotics, image recognition, and language recognition. [Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/neural-network "Learn more about Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/expert-system "Learn more about expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b29), AI can assist Digital Twins by providing an advanced analytical tool capable of automatically analyzing obtained data and providing valuable insights, making predictions about outcomes, and giving suggestions as to how to avoid potential problems [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). 
*   4.[Extended Reality (XR)](https://softengi.com/expertise/expanded-reality/) is an umbrella term used to describe immersive technologies like Virtual Reality (VR), [Augmented Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about Augmented Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR), and Mixed Reality (MR). These technologies can merge the physical and virtual worlds and extend the reality we experience [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b30). XR creates digital representations of objects where digital and real-world objects co-exist and interact in real-time. Digital Twins utilize XR capabilities to digitally model physical objects, allowing users to interact with digital content.

4. Digital twins use cases and applications
-------------------------------------------

Today, engineering and manufacturing predominantly use Digital Twins to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. Digital Twins applications in operations and supply chain management, especially the role of Digital Twins in terms of operations traceability, transport maintenance, remote assistance, asset visualization, and design [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are reviewed in related publications [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38). The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in other industries, including automotive, aerospace, construction, agriculture, mining, utilities, retail, healthcare, military, [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and public safety sectors ([Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig3)) [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b44). The technology has captured the imagination of scholars, managers, and practitioners worldwide, and numerous [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of this technology are emerging in the literature [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b47), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b48), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b49), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b50).

How can Digital Twins improve real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance in construction?
==============================================================================================

Ellen Flannery|17 July, 2024

**Kevin Soohoo, Senior Director of the AEC Practice at Egnyte**

![Image 4](https://www.intelligentbuild.tech/wp-content/uploads/sites/63/2024/07/KEVIN-SOOHOO.png)

Kevin Soohoo, Senior Director of the AEC Practice at Egnyte

Digital Twins enable construction companies to model and analyse physical properties in perpetuity. The benefits of a Digital Twin are quickly transitioning from a nice to have to a necessity for companies doing work that demands efficiency, accuracy and compliance with evolving regulatory standards. Implementing Digital Twins in projects enhances project control by helping companies achieve sustainability goals, adhere to regulatory guidelines and more.

Integrating Digital Twins with the Building Information Modelling (BIM) process empowers firms to address key industry challenges such as reduced productivity, profitability and performance.

During construction, real-time monitoring systems can track progress, identify potential issues and analyse the performance of various systems for quick decision-making and problem resolution. Additionally, Digital Twins allow for the simulation of different construction scenarios and the analysis of potential outcomes. This predictive analytics capability helps anticipate issues, optimise construction processes and improve overall project efficiency.

Digital Twins foster better collaboration among stakeholders by providing a shared, up-to-date view of the project. This enhances communication and coordination across different teams and disciplines. Ensuring that files are the most current versions and reducing duplication makes it easier to locate information and avoid data sprawl. Effective version control and data management will become increasingly important as regulators mandate comprehensive safety regulations requiring extensive record retention.

Beyond the construction phase, Digital Twins provide valuable data for the maintenance and operation of buildings. This supports proactive maintenance, reduces downtime and extends the building’s lifespan. Digital Twins also benefit companies that must comply with safety regulations like the Building Safety Act by retaining accurate and detailed records of their projects long after completion. A realistic project rendering enables firms responsible for monitoring construction, maintenance, and ongoing safety compliance to perform these tasks efficiently and effectively.

**Jeremy Suard, CEO and Co-founder at Exodigo**

![Image 5](https://www.intelligentbuild.tech/wp-content/uploads/sites/63/2024/07/JEREMY-SUARD-1024x1024.jpg)

Jeremy Suard, CEO and Co-founder at Exodigo

Digital Twins play a crucial role in construction safety and efficient use of project resources. A key element that is often overlooked in terms of ongoing monitoring and predictive maintenance is the need to understand what lies beneath the surface.

The unknown of the underground creates massive challenges for construction teams – from potentially dangerous utility strikes early in the build, to costly, unplanned rerouting work along the way or unstable foundations in the future. A powerful combination of advanced sensors, 3D imaging and AI technologies can now create detailed Digital Twins of the underground landscape that can scale to meet changing needs so project leaders can see the full picture, including what lies beneath the surface and properly plan ahead.

Underground Digital Twin technology is revolutionising the construction industry by providing a precise view of the underground – utility lines, bedrock and other potential challenges or changes. Armed with this critical data, construction managers can make informed decisions about what and where to build, while reducing risks, limiting redesigns and minimising delays due to uncharted obstacles.

A leader in underground Digital Twin technology, Exodigo’s multi-sensing underground mapping platform empowers construction managers to plan infrastructure projects that are delivered on time and within budget – fostering sustainable growth and development.

By building smarter, we can avoid problems instead of reacting to them. With a digital record, underground information is perpetually available to any organisation stakeholder. Digital Twins simplify the often challenging task of institutional knowledge transfer in our industry.

**Michael Evans, Senior Director, Product Structures Division, Trimble**

![Image 6](https://www.intelligentbuild.tech/wp-content/uploads/sites/63/2024/07/Michael-evans-trimble-FINAL.jpg)

Michael Evans, Senior Director, Product Structures Division, Trimble

Digital Twins are the way to connect the physical and digital worlds. BIM models of assets comprise federated models from different trades: architecture, engineering, fabrication, construction, MEP and more can be further enriched with data from 3D scans and connected devices (IoT) and constructible, real-time data to turn as-built models into Digital Twins.

Elevating 3D models to Digital Twins increases analytical capabilities throughout all phases of a construction project. Digital Twins offer a better way — a visual common ground that offers invaluable cohesion through which construction phases can communicate more effectively. Component dimensions, model details, working conditions and more can be embedded into content-enabled models. Applying IoT devices delivering up-to-date information to their digital equivalent, the 3D model is then enabled to directly represent that physical asset on the spot. This helps monitor asset status.

All too often, important details get lost in translation as a model progresses down the chain of command. Digital Twin technology offers accurate, real-time updates of building progress that will reveal any deviations from 3D models. Trimble solutions make on-the-spot data collection and design verification faster and less resource-intensive.

Having the most up-to-date information on products or fabricated pieces of a structure (like the source of the material, the certification of the welds, etc.) embedded into content-enabled models allows for ‘Digital Twin builds’ — the quickest, most efficient and most accurate fabrication possible. Trimble can also collect this level of constructed/fabricated/manufactured data which would make the Digital Twin richer when Trimble products are used.

This approach reduces errors and rework, along with the time and money they waste. And, on-site scheduling is enhanced through real just-in-time production and delivery of fabricated components.

When the project is complete, Digital Twins can be handed over to the client to help support ongoing structural enhancements. For example, if a client is interested in the environmental impact of their structure, a Digital Twin can track energy demand, CO2 emissions and indoor air quality to help shape future green initiatives. A Digital Twin can be a value-added asset for clients looking to leverage data in future optimisation projects.

As for existing buildings or infrastructure, Digital Twins can be applied as a starting point for proactive maintenance. This starts with the creation of high-resolution 3D scans through by using mobile mapping systems or 3D (laser) scanners and applying AI-enabled technology to identify structural issues. The next step involves deploying the software to design sustainable mitigative measures based on construction technology that supports high-accuracy location-based construction. With that, the possibility unfolds to create a live Digital Twin that can serve as the basis for future maintenance, with facilities and maintenance data added to the twin on an ongoing basis, to support the asset during its entire lifecycle.

Digital Twins are, first and foremost, analytical tools that offer unprecedented insight into the condition of field investments and help inform future operations.

**Hamid Alemohammad, Chief Operating Officer at Brickeye**

![Image 7](https://www.intelligentbuild.tech/wp-content/uploads/sites/63/2024/07/Hamid_1__2_-edited.png)

Hamid Alemohammad, Chief Operating Officer at Brickeye

Real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance are critical considerations during the lifecycle of construction projects, from pre- to post-construction. The implementation of a Digital Twin can help construction stakeholders better understand and protect their project assets to make insightful and informed predictions on future performance trends, increasing efficiency, meeting compliance requirements and enhancing site productivity based on historical, current and predictive data.

As construction proceeds through the project lifecycle, different types of data are continuously captured – via on-site sensors and monitoring devices, combined with metadata obtained from other sources – allowing for a digital replica to be updated in real-time. Throughout construction, stakeholders can use this Digital Twin platform to gain a multi-dimensional view of the performance of the construction site – predicting trends, detecting risks, setting up automation for risk controls and optimising project schedules.

With the addition of real-time monitoring, Digital Twins mitigates construction risk through early detection of discrepancies – keeping project stakeholders updated on any potential abnormal conditions, incidents or tasks. Before the advent of digital technologies, frequent physical site observations and inspections were required to verify work done and determine the project status. With a Digital Twin platform and other on-site technologies, such as sensors and cameras, augmented data can verify the progress in real-time, without needing to step foot on the job site.

Other benefits include:

*   **Improved worker safety:** Digital Twins enable companies to identify risk-associated areas to prevent unsafe activities and incidents in hazardous zones, and to ensure safe and effective usage of materials.

*   **Enhanced sustainability and efficiency:** Real-time data is collected and analysed to generate insights into equipment utilisation, material tracking and energy consumption.

*   **Greener construction:** Digital Twins can examine and predict the carbon footprint of different aspects of construction projects, offering insights on an ongoing basis about potential process improvements to reduce emissions.

Over the past several years, Digital Twin technology has made significant strides to modernise the construction industry and improve project construction with real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. Unlocking an advanced level of automation allows stakeholders to optimise processes, improve operational efficiency and mitigate risks. With comprehensive data and analytics, Digital Twins can aid in reducing waste, delays and reworks – while prioritising worker safety and sustainability in the wake of climate change – giving stakeholders the information and tools they need to make better-informed decisions to protect their projects.

The Benefits of Digital Twin Technology in Construction
=======================================================

The construction industry embraces digital twin technology to improve project outcomes and streamline operations. Digital twins allow stakeholders to visualize, analyze, and optimize every project phase, bringing unprecedented accuracy that enables teams to detect potential issues early and mitigate costly errors.

With digital twins, construction companies can monitor real-time data, such as structural integrity, environmental conditions, and equipment performance. This data-driven approach prevents downtime, reduces waste, and ensures safety compliance. Additionally, digital twins also enhance collaboration between architects, engineers, and construction teams by providing a shared, up-to-date project model.

From smarter resource management to better communication and more informed decision-making, here is a look at the [benefits of digital twins](https://www.attinc.com/news/digital-twin-vs-building-information-modeling-bim/) in construction.

What is a Digital Twin for Construction?
----------------------------------------

A digital twin for construction is a virtual replica of a physical building, infrastructure, or construction process that mirrors its real-world counterpart in every detail. This digital representation integrates data from sensors, 3D modeling, and real-time analytics, providing an in-depth understanding of how the physical structure or system is performing.

It’s important to recognize that this representation goes beyond static blueprints, it becomes a dynamic tool that adapts and evolves based on real-time data. This allows for predictive maintenance, improved resource allocation, and enhanced decision-making throughout the project lifecycle.

With digital twins, construction projects can be better managed from planning to completion, reducing errors, improving efficiency, and delivering more sustainable outcomes. This innovation transforms how the industry approaches design, construction, and maintenance, ensuring greater precision and reduced costs.

How Does the Construction Industry Implement Digital Twins?
-----------------------------------------------------------

The implementation of digital twins in construction begins at the planning and design stages and extends through the entire project lifecycle. The digital twin serves as a centralized model that all stakeholders, architects, engineers, contractors, and facility managers can access to collaborate more efficiently and make informed decisions.

Applying digital twins in construction requires the integration of advanced technologies, including IoT, cloud computing, artificial intelligence (AI), and machine learning (ML). These technologies work together to create an interactive and responsive virtual environment that mirrors the real-world project in every detail.

These technologies enable the application of digital twin technology in the following construction workflows:

*   **Design and Planning:**In the early stages, digital twins visualize the [construction project in 3D](https://www.attinc.com/services/construction-laser-scanning/), enabling stakeholders to experiment with design options, identify flaws, and make real-time adjustments. This helps prevent costly errors before the project begins.
*   **Construction and Execution:**During construction, digital twins track progress by synchronizing with the physical site via IoT sensors. They provide real-time data on structural integrity, equipment performance, and environmental conditions, allowing for informed decisions that keep the project on schedule and within budget.
*   **Maintenance and Post-Construction:**After completion, digital twins monitor building systems, using AI to predict maintenance needs, reduce downtime, and extend the building’s lifespan by detecting potential issues before they become critical.

What are the Benefits of Digital Twins in Construction?
-------------------------------------------------------

The benefits of digital twins in construction extend across all phases of a project, from design and planning to execution and long-term maintenance. 

Here are some of the key advantages to note:

### **Enhanced Project Visualization**

One of the primary benefits of a digital twin in construction is its ability to enhance project visualization. Digital twins offer dynamic, real-time 3D models of buildings or infrastructure, enabling stakeholders to visualize projects comprehensively.

For instance, during the construction of the Museum of the Future in Dubai, digital twin technology was employed to [simulate various design elements](https://www.asce.org/publications-and-news/civil-engineering-source/civil-engineering-magazine/issues/magazine-issue/article/2023/01/museum-of-the-future-sets-standard-for-whats-to-come-in-digital-design), allowing architects and clients to understand the finished structure before construction commenced.

### **Improved Collaboration**

In the development of [Singapore’s digital twin, _Virtual Singapore_,](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtual_Singapore) various agencies combined their efforts into a common platform. Through this platform, architects, engineers, and contractors can view and interact with the same digital model in real-time, allowing for more seamless communication and quicker resolution of any issues that arise during the construction phase.

This enhanced collaboration ensures that all team members are working from the most accurate and up-to-date information, minimizing miscommunications and reducing the risk of costly mistakes. Ultimately, this leads to faster project completion and a smoother overall workflow.

### **Real-Time Monitoring and Predictive Maintenance**

Digital twins offer powerful tools for real-time monitoring of construction processes and completed structures. IoT sensors in building materials capture data on factors like structural integrity, temperature, and environmental impact. This real-time data helps construction teams make informed decisions and adjust plans efficiently.

After construction, digital twins enable predictive maintenance by monitoring building systems such as HVAC, electrical, and plumbing. They identify potential issues early, reducing downtime, cutting maintenance costs, and extending the building’s lifespan while maintaining optimal performance.

### **Cost Optimization**

One of the most significant benefits of digital twins in construction is their ability to optimize costs throughout the project lifecycle. Digital twins for buildings help construction teams identify areas where they can reduce waste and allocate resources more effectively.

By providing real-time insights into material usage, energy consumption, and labor efficiency, digital twins help optimize costs throughout the project lifecycle. In the Crossrail project in London, now known as the Elizabeth Line, digital twins were used to [simulate and optimize construction processes](https://rail.nridigital.com/future_rail_jul21/digital_twin_crossrail), leading to significant cost savings during construction and throughout the infrastructure’s operational life.

### **Increased Safety**

Safety is a top priority in construction, and digital twins play a crucial role in enhancing safety on job sites. Digital twins help identify hazards before they lead to accidents by simulating potential risks. They also provide real-time data on structural stability, environmental conditions, and equipment performance, which enables engineers to monitor stress levels in the building’s framework.

The application of digital twin technology during the [construction of Heathrow Airport’s Terminal 1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_twin) is a great example of this. Movement sensors installed in the cofferdam and boreholes provided real-time data, which was integrated into a digital object model. This integration enabled engineers to visualize and assess structural movements, facilitating timely preventive measures to ensure safety and stability.

With real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance capabilities, digital twins help construction teams make informed decisions that improve project timelines and reduce risks.

By analyzing real-time data, digital twins can forecast maintenance needs, enabling proactive repairs that minimize downtime and extend the lifespan of infrastructure assets.
Digital twins provide stakeholders with detailed simulations and insights, allowing for informed decision-making throughout the project lifecycle, from design to construction and maintenance.
Combining IoT for data collection and AI for analysis, digital twins optimize performance, enhance predictive analytics, and improve operational efficiency across various infrastructure projects.
